1: sigē en tō ouranō hōs hēmimōrion — "silence in heaven for about half an hour." The silence is without precedent in Revelation's preceding heavenly scenes, which have been filled with the voices of living creatures, elders, and myriads of angels. The silence is liturgical — it parallels the practice of silence before solemn pronouncements in Jewish and later Christian worship (Habakkuk 2:20: "YHWH is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent before him"; Zephaniah 1:7; Zechariah 2:13). The "half an hour" is one of Revelation's rare time-specifications, marking the solemnity of what follows.
2: hoi hepta angeloi hoi enōpion tou Theou — "the seven angels who stand before God." These are the archangels of Jewish tradition (1 Enoch Revelation 20:1-8 names Uriel, Raphael, Raguel, Michael, Saraqael, Gabriel, and Remiel as the seven holy angels "who present the prayers of the saints and enter into the presence of the glory of the Holy One"). The trumpets given to them are judicial instruments — in OT warfare the trumpets were blown before battle (Numbers 10:9; Joshua 6:4-5).
3: libano pollo — "a large amount of incense." The incense altar before the throne corresponds to the golden altar of incense in the Tabernacle and Temple, positioned before the Holy of Holies. tais proseuchais tōn hagiōn pantōn — "with the prayers of all the saints." The incense is not merely liturgical decoration but is given to be offered "with" the prayers — the angels' offering amplifies and presents the accumulated prayers of God's people. The grammar makes the incense and prayers co-offerings before God.
4: anebē ho kapnos tōn thymiamatōn — "the smoke of the incense went up" — the direction is key: prayers ascend to God. The prayers of the saints have been accumulating (cf. Revelation 6:9-11: the martyr-souls crying out, "how long?"); now they ascend as incense, setting in motion the judgment-sequence of the trumpets. The passive structure signals divine reception: God receives the prayers and acts in response.
5: eilēphen ton libanōton kai egemisen auton ek tou pyros tou thysiastēriou — the same censer that held the incense-with-prayers is now filled with fire from the altar and hurled down. The movement is exact: prayers ascend, judgment-fire descends. brontai kai phōnai kai astrapai kai seismos (thunder, rumblings, lightning, earthquake) — the Sinai theophany-formula (Exodus 19:16-18), which recurs at each of Revelation's great climaxes (Revelation 11:19; Revelation 16:18).
6: The seven angels prepare (ētoimasan, aorist: completed preparation) to sound. The verb is not "picked up" but "prepared" — a deliberate act of readiness before execution. The symmetry with Revelation 7 (sealing before trumpets) is structural: the servants of God are sealed before the judgment-trumpets sound, ensuring the protection of 7:3 is in place.
7: chalaza kai pyr memigmena en haimati — "hail and fire mixed with blood." The hail recalls Exodus 9:22-26 (seventh Egyptian plague); the fire recalls Joel 2:30 ("I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke"); the blood adds a third element absent from Exod 9. The mixture creates a compound plague-sign that draws on multiple OT registers simultaneously. to triton (a third) — the partial judgment (one-third affected) distinguishes the trumpets from the bowls (total judgment); the scale is warning-not-yet-final.
8: hōs oros mega pyrī kaiomenon — "something like a great mountain blazing with fire was thrown into the sea." The simile-hedge (hōs, like) is typical of John's visionary caution: he does not claim to describe the thing itself but its appearance. The blazing mountain recalls Jeremiah 51:25 ("Behold I am against you, O destroying mountain... I will make you a burned-out mountain"). egeneto eis haima — became blood (not "turned blood-red" but ontological transformation, as in the Exodus plague).
9: ta echonta psychas (those having souls/lives) — the phrase emphasizes that these are living creatures, not merely organisms; the destruction is of ensouled life, not just biological matter. diephtharesan (aorist passive: were destroyed/corrupted) — the same root as phtheirō (to corrupt, destroy) used for the corruption of the false teachers throughout the epistles. The judgment-vocabulary links the moral and the cosmic.
10: astēr megas kaiomenos hōs lampas — "a great star blazing like a torch." The torch-simile (lampas, not lychnia) suggests brilliant, streaming fire rather than a contained flame. epesan ek tou ouranou — "fell from heaven" (aorist); the falling star echoes Isaiah 14:12 ("How you have fallen from heaven, morning star, son of the dawn") and Luke 10:18 ("I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven"). The connection to fallen angelic power is interpretively possible but not made explicit by the text.
11: Apsinthos — wormwood (Artemisia absinthium), an extremely bitter plant; in the OT, wormwood (la'anah) symbolizes divine judgment through bitter experience (Jeremiah 9:15: "I will feed this people with wormwood and give them bitter water to drink"; Lamentations 3:15,19; Proverbs 5:4). The name "Wormwood" given to the star is not found in the OT; John creates the designation to match the effect. eplēthysan (aorist passive: were made bitter) — the waters become their punishment.
12: eplēgē to triton tou hēliou — "a third of the sun was struck." The passive eplēgē (was struck) uses divine-passive construction; the agent is unstated. The "third" (triton) motif runs through the trumpet sequence — partial rather than total disruption, distinguishing the trumpets from the final bowl-judgments. The darkening of sun, moon, and stars recalls the Egyptian darkness-plague (Exodus 10:21) and Joel 2:10 and Amos 8:9 ("I will make the sun go down at noon").
13: aetos petomenon en mesouranēmati — "an eagle flying in midheaven." The eagle as a prophetic messenger recalls Deuteronomy 28:49 (the eagle as judgment from afar) and Hosea 8:1 ("an eagle over the house of the LORD"). ouai ouai ouai — the triple woe, corresponding to the three remaining trumpets. The triplication of the woe-formula is intensification by repetition, a Hebraic rhetorical device. The transition at v13 divides the seven trumpets into two groups: 1-4 (cosmic) and 5-7 (demonic/historical), with the woe-eagle marking the break.
